region_of_origin: India (North Indian / Mughlai tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Reshmi Chicken Kebab; closest match is the generic chicken kebab food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Reshmi Chicken Kebab. A0FJP (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Reshmi Chicken Kebab or a sufficiently close prepared chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Reshmi Chicken Kebab is approximately 150 g, comprising 3 to 4 skewered pieces of grilled chicken breast.

nutrient_source.title: Reshmi Chicken Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Reshmi Chicken Kebab (cr_id G00010929). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical record. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for cream- and cashew-based chicken preparations (~8.5 g/100g) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on marinated grilled chicken with yogurt and low-carbohydrate marinade
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Reshmi Chicken Kebab. GI is estimated at 35 based on the analogous food category of marinated grilled chicken with yogurt-based marinade and low net carbohydrate content (5.24 g/100g). Yogurt has a GI of approximately 36 (University of Sydney GI Database); the dominant protein and fat matrix of this dish suppresses postprandial glucose response. GL per 150 g serving = (35 × 5.24 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.75, rounded to 3.1 accounting for marinade sugars. This is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with high-protein, high-fat grilled meat preparations.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — nearly double the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Reshmi Chicken Kebab due to its very high sodium content of 1080 mg/100g; if consumed, restrict to a small portion (≤50 g) and avoid additional high-sodium accompaniments such as chutneys or pickles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) is favourable, but high total fat (26.4 g/100g) including saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Reshmi Chicken Kebab has a low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate content making it acceptable for blood glucose management, but the high saturated fat from cream and cashew paste should be monitored — limit to one serving (≤150 g) and pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than high-GI breads or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) and very high sodium (1080 mg/100g) both elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should consume Reshmi Chicken Kebab only occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of high saturated fat from cream and cashew paste and very high sodium significantly raises LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~8.5 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold; sodium 1080 mg/100g worsens tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Reshmi Chicken Kebab is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to high saturated fat content that promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, compounded by very high sodium that exacerbates joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Reshmi Chicken Kebab (Hindi: रेशमी चिकन कबाब, meaning 'silky chicken kebab') is a North Indian Mughlai-origin grilled chicken dish popular across Indian restaurants in Singapore and South Asia. It is prepared from boneless chicken breast marinated in a rich mixture of yogurt (curd), fresh cream, cashew nut paste, egg, and aromatic spices including cardamom, white pepper, and garam masala, then grilled in a tandoor (clay oven) at high heat. The result is a tender, silky, mildly spiced kebab with a creamy, slightly smoky flavour and a soft, juicy texture — distinct from the drier, more charred seekh or tikka kebabs. Per 100 g, it provides 302 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 26.4 g fat (of which approximately 8.5 g is saturated fat from cream and cashew), and only 5.24 g carbohydrate, making it a low-carbohydrate but calorie-dense and high-fat preparation. Sodium is very high at 1080 mg/100g, making it unsuitable for hypertension or cardiovascular disease management without significant portion restriction. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), driven by the protein-fat matrix suppressing postprandial glucose response. Clinically, it is acceptable in small portions for Type 2 Diabetes management but should be avoided or strictly limited by individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sodium and saturated fat content. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a starter or main course at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls, often accompanied by mint chutney and sliced onions.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Reshmi Chicken Kebab (cr_id G00010929, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN data for comparable cream- and cashew-based chicken preparations. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for yogurt-marinated grilled chicken with low net carbohydrate content.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (8.5 g/100g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN reference data for cream- and cashew-based chicken preparations; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific tandoor-grilled preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is a confirmed zero from the approved source — the marinade and chicken breast contribute negligible fibre. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Reshmi Chicken Kebab was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 5. Halal status is assumed based on standard restaurant preparation with halal-certified chicken; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 6. The MUFA/PUFA carve-out for nuts does not apply here as cream is the primary fat source in a prepared dish context.
